item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hate to be 'that guy' because the article is well written and leaves the reader wondering what the item is and how it was made, but..
Wouldn't the object class be 'Safe' since we understand how to keep it contained (and calm, no less)
and why would The Foundation spend so much on what is essentially a moody rock? I understand the illusions would cause some confusion at any non-dedicated Foundation site…but wouldn't it be cheaper to just lock it away somewhere far enough away it wouldn't bother anyone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree. On my first read I assumed that everyone was affected in its "sphere" of influence, i.e. wherever the illusions show up. This would be quite troublesome to have everyone in a 100m radius constantly worried something bad is going to happen. Having hundreds of red, 4 eyed ghost things pilling up would be quite disruptive to work and the state of mind of the scientists and their visibility, as well as other SCPs on the site that might be aware of their presence. Also, why not have all the things in the same room? Will it not work?

Maybe it needs some small rewriting to make its containment procedures more valid, or the other way around.

EDIT: Whops, sorry for the late date, I need to make more of a habit of checking when things are posted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weirdest discovery ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the purposes of the SCP Board Game, Escape from site 19, we have made a photograph of SCP-1410. The author can legally use it per his discretion, if he ever has to remove his image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Considering the whole story this is a fine article, but
1) why did the researchers not measure and weigh the stone, and
2) were there any attemtps of prespiration sample analysis?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but I too am bugged by the resources being thrown into a relatively easily contained SCP.

Reading through the article, it seems that only Red instances would cause a problem for the foundation. The other illusions could be easily contained by simply keeping 1410 in a decent sized room with D-Class actors posted as "guards".

The foundation could use the time previously spent keeping 1410 happy researching how to limit the more troublesome illusions.

I really like the idea otherwise, and I think I could be convinced if there were just a little stronger justification - for example, observers beginning to succumb to the same delusions as 1410, or the illusions gradually becoming corporeal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But they are still just illusions.
Set it off to one side in a non-critical archival wing and post a sign to ignore the harmless phantasms. I'd think that ghosts that don't drive you crazy or poltergeist you into walls would be a refreshing break compared to what most Foundation staff are used to.
Actually, it's already got its own converted auditorium. That should be plenty of space for a whole metric butt-ton of red watchers.

Additionaly, this just occured to me, the current containment calls for staff to repeatedly expose themselves to an only somewhat understood mind-affecting anomaly on a regular basis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Red instances of SCP-1410-1 will attempt to observe SCP-1410 from a distance, often appearing several hundred meters from SCP-1410."

"SCP-1410 does not seem to be aware that it is the source of SCP-1410-1, and will react negatively to them, increasing its rate of perspiration and thus the creation of additional instances of SCP-1410-1 the longer it is in contact with them."

The illusions aren't a problem by themselves, but if you don't take care to keep their numbers low, you'll end up with a whole bunch of the things, and with the Red instances sometimes appearing fairly far from SCP-1410 itself, that could quickly become a problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That irked me to, but just a little. I still enjoyed reading.
The illusions seem to be not a problem for anyone/thing but the rock, and limited to the space it currently occupies. So if we stop bothering to reassure it, what's the down-side? The rock won't be happy, but what does the Foundation care about that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems really not like the Foundation to provide all of this attention, personnel, and resources to a rock.

It just doesn't make sense to me why they're going so far out of the way to make sure it's nice and comfy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gave this an upvote, because I thought it was interesting enough, original, and well-executed, but I'm not sure if I think it's the best it could be. Whether that's because I'm still not crazy about the concept, or because I feel this didn't have as much effort put into it as you could have, I can't tell you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: E: Rethinking it, might be better as a whole different SCP. I'll have to consider it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: E: No longer relevant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sounds interesting. I'll be sure to give it a look. In the meantime, I'm thinking of basing my article on a few articles I have, mostly focusing on the dietary aspect of the theory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show
- Hide

Close, overall. A group of artists makes something that royally pisses off the Foundation, and hid in a cave complex. One of them begins to get affected by the Ichor, until he goes mad and starts doing its/her bidding, which is to create art from the body parts of his fellows. After the Ichor gets bored of him and his arts, she turns him into 1410, and embeds it into the wall of Site-30, for her own reasons. The Blue Subtype are indeed the memory of the artist's family, but he never got to see them again after feeling to the cave. Whatever is left of him misses them, but realizes it went too far and got too twisted (which is the Green realization) to ever be with them again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you're really passionate about the Humours, there's this novel by Rupert Thomson - "Divided Kingdom" that uses the humours as a social stratifier.
I honestly didn't like the book, maybe because it may be tied too much to criticisms of modern British government. But it's an interesting experiment on modern literary implementation of the Four Humours theory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's pretty much it. I wrote the thing specifically so it can also be read on its own, but if you go for the arc-dependent view, that's the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ooh, I know which one this is referencing.

+ show block
– hide block

It's the narrator of that particular tale, transformed after the 'She' behind it was removed. It's now stuck reliving its old memories, constrained by the limits of the form it was trapped in — as some sort of performance art, perhaps, in response to AWCY?'s pointless drivel. The Blood of the World deemed it fit to deliver to the Foundation for some purposes of its own.

I wouldn't know what to think of it on its own, but it really ties nicely into that article and what's behind it. I has an enjoyment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let me think…

+ show block
– hide block

Counter-culture artist (possibly involved in the anomalous scene, but not necessary) gets caught doing something, chased by police/authority figures wearing gasmasks. Artists hides in a cave, gets drawn in by the mind-effecting Ichor. The Ichor fucks with his brain to the extent that he feels artwork made of mutilated human body parts is fascinating and beautiful, and the artist then acts on this, bringing people to his cave, mutilating them, and making art for the Ichor. The Ichor gets bored, and turns the artist into a rock with variable/shattered sapience. The artist is brought back to his home somehow - he might have been home when the change happened - and his children ignored him, because they didn't know the rock was their father. Rock somehow ends up outside the Foundation. Red is the chase, Yellow is the creation stage, Green is the horror at the realization of what he's done, and Blue is him trying to reach out to his children.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funny you should mention that, since I actually have another SCP in mind that utilizes them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had a bit of cognitive dissonance because I immediately tried to tie this to the Four Humours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll say this much- the different colors and apparitions represent stages in a traumatic event, the one that led to the creation of 1410 in the first place. Try going Red, Yellow, Green, Blue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm…. Dunno what to think about this one. Bit too much going on to digest all at once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this, though it is tough to pinpoint why it appeals to me. I guess it has something to do with my preference towards SCPs that require as much care as containment. You've got my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has been an interesting article to write. Careful readers may find connection with another article of mine hidden there.
